text: The surgeon carefully dissects the omentum, which is densely adhered to the necrotic gallbladder. As the omentum is peeled away, dark brown bile is visible leaking from the gallbladder, indicating severe cholecystitis. A benign liver cyst is also observed.

The surgeon uses ICG imaging to identify the liver border while carefully dissecting the omentum plastered over the liver and gallbladder. The omentum is peeled away from the inflamed right upper quadrant abscess and cholecystitis, revealing the extent of infection. Retraction is applied to lift the gallbladder, exposing the massively infected area.

The surgeon continues to carefully dissect the omentum from the liver using blunt techniques, guided by ICG to avoid liver injury. The dissection progresses towards the infundibulum, where the necrosis is severe, revealing liquefied tissue.

The surgeon uses a suction irrigator to dissect the necrotic and gangrenous gallbladder, revealing liquefactive tissue. ICG is employed to identify anatomical structures, successfully highlighting the duodenum. The surgeon carefully removes the thick, inflamed omentum from the duodenum, exposing the chronic inflammation.

The surgeon begins a subtotal cholecystectomy by carefully removing the surface of the gallbladder from the cystic plate, staying high above the infundibulum. A large stone is visible stuck in the infundibulum as the majority of the gallbladder face is dissected and set aside.

The surgeon continues to dissect the wall of the gallbladder, approaching a tubular structure. A window is visible on the backside, showing the cystic plate and liver. The structure appears to lead directly to the gallbladder, prompting the surgeon to clip and ligate it.
The surgeon ligates a structure believed to be the cystic artery, observing the stump that confirms its appearance. Subsequently, more of the gallbladder wall is dissected away from the cystic plate.

The surgeon maintains a high position above the sulcus while accessing the gallbladder towards the infundibulum. The impacted stone is visible, and the surgeon attempts to flush it out. Using suction, the stone is eventually dislodged. The surgeon then irrigates the cystic duct and infundibulum to remove any remaining gravel or sludge.

The surgeon inspects the gallbladder for additional stones before turning attention to the right upper quadrant. A large abscess is visible, occupying the entire anterior surface of the liver and extending posteriorly to the right hemidiaphragm. The surgeon debrides and drains the thick, chronic abscess cavity.

The surgeon debrides the abscess cavity from the liver in the right upper quadrant, removing necrotic tissue. For the subtotal cholecystectomy, the surgeon uses a monopolar cautery to ablate the mucosa of the gallbladder.
The surgeon places the stone, multiple pieces of rind from the abscess cavity, and the gallbladder from the subtotal cholecystectomy into an endocatch bag for removal.

The surgeon places a hemostatic agent in the cystic plate and infundibulum to aid in hemostasis and promote scarring of the cystic duct. Two drains are positioned: one in the right upper quadrant to drain the abscess and another in the cystic plate to monitor for bile leakage following the subtotal cholecystectomy.
